top_categories.workout_context: When someone attacks on mixed terrain, you have 10 seconds to respond or you're off the back. This builds that response.
Race-pace efforts that mimic GFNY Salinas' demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before March.
Base fitness for GFNY Salinas. Without this, the hard workouts break you down instead of building you up.
65 miles on mixed terrain burns energy faster than the distance suggests. Train the fade resistance now.
GFNY Salinas' mixed terrain rewards steady threshold power. Surging and recovering costs more watts than just holding.
GFNY Salinas' mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 65 miles.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of GFNY Salinas. Train it until it feels boring.
Long rides with hard efforts mixed in. Simulates what GFNY Salinas' mixed terrain actually does to you.
At GFNY Salinas, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
Double-threshold training builds sustained power with less fatigue. More FTP per hour of training.
Below LT1, you burn fat. Above it, you burn glycogen. For 65 miles, a higher LT1 means slower bonk.
2-minute all-out efforts on mixed terrain. Closing gaps, responding to attacks, sprinting for position. Train the burn.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 40–60g carbs/hour for GFNY Salinas’s 65 miles. Front-load calories in the first half. One bottle per hour minimum, more in heat.
race_overlay.altitude: GFNY Salinas with 2,881ft of climbing, much of it above 8,000ft demands altitude preparation. Arrive 5–7 days early for acclimatization. Expect 5–15% power reduction at altitude. Increase iron intake 4 weeks out. Hydrate aggressively — altitude increases fluid loss by 20–40%.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on VO2max, Race Simulation, and Endurance to prepare you for 65 miles of mixed terrain in Salinas, Santa Elena, Ecuador.
